sentences.text: I believe that the funding for the soup kitchen, at least it began with the Jewish community of Lisbon.
Both the hospital and the soup kitchen were side by side and I believe it went back to the 1930s, at least, when the hostel was built.
And as to how my Aunt Esther became involved, again, I'm assuming that she lived next door to Mr.
Torjman, who was one of the cooks in there.
And also Mr.
Torjman was in charge of the kosher meat distribution in Lisbon for the Jewish community.
His wife was my Hebrew teacher, personal tutor of Hebrew.
And so-- so they were friends and I'm assuming that because of Mr.
Torjman being at a soup kitchen, that he then somehow talked my Aunt Esther to work, as well, there and manage the soup kitchen.
But primarily, it was built for the poor of the community.
And I would say not just Jewish, there were other-- other members of-- that used to come by and no one was ever neglected a meal, so to speak.

And later on, as the-- at the beginning of the war, Second World War and the Jewish refugees started to really coming to Lisbon, then they-- they divided two-- two halls and the largest hall became then the hall for the-- the dining room for the refugees.
It would probably seat within 120, 150 people.
And the other small dining room became the one used by the few Portuguese Jews, the poor of the community.
So I used to go just to help my Aunt Esther.
We had a great relationship and I loved to listen to all the different languages.
Because at the time, I spoke Portuguese, a little bit Latino, a few words here and there of French, and in fact, a few words of [?
Magarbe, ?] which was the dialect spoke by the-- by the Moroccan Jews.

But one day, or I should say perhaps one noon, I was there working, helping with the setup of the tables, and I noticed that a family walked in very well-dressed, not like the refugees which were dressed in drab clothing and so forth.
But what really prompted me to raise a question was they were speaking fluent Portuguese.
So I approached the man and I said, excuse me, sir, in Portuguese, this dining room is for the refugees.
The Portuguese dining room is on the other side.
So the man kind of laughed a little and he said, we, too, are refugees.
And-- and that was a little bit puzzling to me because I was a young man, teenager, I-- I knew there was a war going on.
You heard-- you read the papers, you heard comments at the synagogue, et cetera and I was surprised when he said that we, too, are refugees.
So I ask him.
I was very questionable.
I said, how is is it that you are refugees?
You are Portuguese.
And he says, sit down, young man.
Sit down.
And I sit down and this man began to tell me a story.
He told me, I'll tell you why I am a refugee.
And he said, my name is Dr.
Aristides de Sousa Mendes, [PORTUGUESE].

I was the Portuguese consul in Bordeaux, France and I was expelled by the Portuguese government from my position because I issued visas to all these people against the orders of the Portuguese Prime Minister Salazar, Antonio de Oliveira Salazar.
And I still couldn't quite understand exactly why should a man of such a stature be expelled because he gave visas?
He was saving lives.
The Portuguese government should be honored to have such a man on their team.

But that was not the case and Dr. de Sousa Mendes actually was-- had received orders from the ambassador of Portugal in London, England at some point in time in the '40s- 1940, perhaps 1941-- not to issue visas to Jews or to members of communities with impure blood-- I believe it was also referring to gypsies-- and not to members of the anti-German movement, if you wish, anti-Nazis, and also not to issue visas to members of some other countries that had been overrun by the Nazis, if it was Belgian or Holland or France or et cetera.

So as a result, Dr. de Sousa Mendes-- he-- he said of very famous words that was told later on by his son to me, that I'd rather be on the side of God against man, as opposed to being on the side of man against God.
And his conscience, as he said, did not allow him to allow so many people to die simply because they were Jews.
And outside the Portuguese Consul in Bordeaux, France, there were hundreds-- hundreds-- of refugees waiting to receive that piece of paper.

Because of the fact that Dr. de Sousa Mendes ignored the orders of the Portuguese government-- disobeyed the orders, as a matter of fact-- the Portuguese government then sent the ambassador of Portugal in Spain to Bordeaux, France, which was the closest, and they retrieved Dr. de Sousa Mendes from his position.

And I guess at one point in time, they ran out of the visas shortly before the ambassador of Spain came in and you wrote visas on playing pieces of paper stating that my name is so and so and so, I am the-- the-- consul of Portugal here and-- in Bordeaux, France, and allow these people-- permit these people to pass through into Portugal.
And I guess one of the places was-- that they passed through was in Irun, in the north-- north border between Spain and France.
At one point in time, the-- some of the-- several areas of the border in the southern part of France had been closed by the Germans in agreement with-- with the Spanish government.
But ultimately, Dr. de Sousa Mendes, some statistics refer to the fact that he probably saved over 30,000 lives, of which well over 10,000 Jewish lives and many others.
Many, many predominant people passed through to his grace.
Interesting enough is that Dr. de Sousa Mendes and his family, they had 14 children.
They were devout Catholics from the north of Portugal.
However, Dr. de Sousa Mendes' family in 1497, they were Jews.
They were forced to convert to Christianity.
So they became the so-called crypto-Jews, if you wish at times referred to as Muranos.
And so I'm certain that-- that he knew about it.
And whether or not-- I really doubt that that was the main reason that he-- he wished to save the Jews because I believe he saved the Jews because it was his character.
His conscience did not allow him to see so many people die because they were taken prisoners by judgments and that would be the end of it.
So-- so I had the great privilege, the great honor, to meet this great man and I'll never forget it.
No.
I-- I had heard earlier that because interesting enough, next door to our house in Lisbon, we had a-- a- a-- a horseshoe man.
He was like, looked like the Jolly Green Giant.
He was a big man with a big belly and his job was horsing shoes.
Next door to him was an equestrian school.
We used to play there when we were children, as well.
And Don Luiz was his name, L-U-I-Z, always asked me when I came back from school to read him the newspapers.
And then other people would come in and sit in this little shop-- two or three other guys, I don't even know their names-- and they were listening as I was reading the newspaper.
Then they used to-- to debate because they-- they were really Portuguese Portuguese, you know.
They came from a town in the north of Portugal by the name of Guimaraes, G-U-I-M-A-R-A-E-S.
And if you go to visit Portugal today and if you visit Guimaraes, you will see the signs that will say [PORTUGUESE], Portugal was born here.
They're very devoted, very, very Portuguese.
So of course, they didn't particularly care about the war, Hitler, the refugees, the destruction et cetera.
And also they didn't care for the fact that there were rumors that the Nazis, the German Army, may even invade Portugal.
And there was always some maneuvers going on.
Of course, that never materialized, but there was always a talk amongst the-- the people that, well, there's a possibility that-- that the war will affect us ultimately because they'll come in.

So the government of Portugal, Salazar, they had sent troops to different parts of Portugal to the frontiers and to the-- for special training, et cetera, et cetera, but it never-- nothing ever happened.

So I heard already, and particularly because I was also a Jew-- in the synagogue there was-- there was-- there was not just natives of Portugal, but you would see there was a few group of Polish Jews that I remember from Lotz, L-O-T-Z.
Because I remember they used to manufacture wool sweaters and sell them in the summer in the beaches of-- outside Lisbon.
I remember that.
And so they spoke already Portuguese, they learned the language.
And you would hear them talk about what goes on, the-- the problems with the-- with the-- the Nazis in exterminating the Jewish people.
So--
No, I don't think so.

As a matter of fact, one of the reasons that I left Portugal was because I wanted to go to-- to Eretz, to Palestine, and to join the army and go and fight the Nazis-- me and my brother, God rest his soul, my cousin, and several other members of the Jewish community.
There was one of the main reasons.
We wanted to go and fight the Germans, fight the Nazis.
Not really.
I can't say that I did hear anything about extermination camps.
I did not.

It's interesting to point out, though, that it was also difficult for me, for some of the other Portuguese native Jews, to understand the communications between the refugees because of the variety of languages they were speaking.
And-- and certainly, I did not speak any of those languages-- at the time, anyway.
But you don't have to understand the language.
In many cases, it was adequate to look at their faces and you could see the sadness.
To begin with, the refugees used to come to the soup kitchen quite a bit early.
They had nothing to do, OK?
So they would be there an hour even before the meals.
And they would set up in groups because the soup kitchen was located on a dead end street.
There was the hospital and next door the soup kitchen.
And-- and you would see groups, small groups of people-- you know, four or five or half a dozen-- standing or some walking.
I remember very definitely people walking just-- they wouldn't lift up their faces.
I mean, they were looking just to the ground, not saying anything.
You would see people crying.
And again, you would see these people debating, heavily debating, strongly debating.
And you could hear the name Hitler, you could hear the name Nazis.
But many times I could not understand.
They were just speaking Yiddish, Polish, Russian, German, and-- and-- and so forth.
So--
Very definitely.
You would see most of the time broken families.
You-- I don't remember very, very large numbers of-- of entire families being together.
Broken families would be the word, yes.
Not knowing what happened to the children, to the father, to the mother, to the grandparents, et cetera.
They were just anxiously waiting for-- for the words which nobody had at the time for them.
But something else happened in Portugal, too, that I'd like to touch.
A great number of these immigrants were obviously people that were academicians, doctors, scientists, engineers, professional people, business people, et cetera.
There was a point in time when if they had the visa, they were allowed to pass through to Portugal, and they were allowed to stay within a given period of time.

I don't know if it was three months or it was six months, but I do remember that after a given period of time, some people would be taken into jail, jailed simply because they were in Portugal illegally.
Their term of staying had elapsed and they couldn't move, so they were taken to jail as prisoners.

And I don't know exactly the details, but I do know that the American Joint Committee-- the Jewish Community of Portugal, the American Joint Committee, the HIAS, the assistant to the Jewish refugees, et cetera, they-- they requested the Portuguese government to allow these people.
After all, they had suffered enough.
To be put in jail with common criminals simply because their-- their visa had elapsed wasn't quite justified.

So there-- there were requests to the Portuguese government to give them some areas where they could stay, wherever, until they would get the visas to-- to leave Portugal so that they would have a dignified stay and dignified life and ease their misery.
So the Portuguese government, I believe, went along with that and they allowed several areas in the neighborhood of Lisbon.
I cannot remember all the names.
It was Figuera de Foz I know was one of those and there were several others.
But they could not-- the conditions was they could stay there.
They arranged for-- for homes for them to stay, but they could not leave without a permit from the Portuguese government, the-- the immigration police.
I remember that.
So ultimately-- ultimately, many-- many Jews were allowed to stay in Portugal and many of them stayed.
Was there a time when they started to think about immigration from Lisbon to Palestine?
Yes.

Because of the fact that there was-- at times, there were quite a few Jewish immigrants in Portugal, in Lisbon, interesting enough, there were times where there were large numbers of children that had been rescued from France and brought through Spain into Portugal.
There was a family in Lisbon by the name of Sequerra, S-E-Q-U-E-R-R-A, and Yoel Sequerra was employed, I believe, by the American Joint Committee.
And he used to-- to work the corridor of France, Spain, Portugal, and sneak in Jewish children.
And-- and again, I believe also that he worked to a certain extent with Dr. de Sousa Mendes in getting visas from Dr. de Sousa Mendes for the children to cross through Spain into Portugal.
So when-- when we left Lisbon aboard the ship N-Y-S-S-A, on January 23rd, 1944, there were probably 300 or 400 children, refugees.
And there were more-- the number of was larger than the adults, as a matter of fact.
And our first stop was in Cadiz, Spain, Port of Cadiz, C-A-D-I-Z.
And aboard came Yoel Sequerra with 360 children that had stayed in Cadiz and they also immigrated to Israel, their parents, to Palestine.
So these things are-- are memories that will never leave you because you can see that there was many times happiness amongst these children.
They were free, they were on the way to Eretz Israel.
There were Jewish-- many times, you could see the Jewish teachers, as well, dancing the oro, the horo, and, and, and singing some Hebrew songs.
Because among some of these children, and particularly I would say Polish Jewish children, they had many, many Hebrew training in Poland even before they left, actually.
And so they knew more Hebrew than anybody else that I remember already.
So there was many happy times aboard the ship.
It took-- took us about three or four days.
There was only a scary moment-- not a moment, I should say perhaps longer than a moment.
We left Cadiz and maybe a day later, we received over the loudspeakers orders from the-- the captain of the not to panic because there was German submarine action in the Mediterranean at the time.
And we saw a submarine, we saw a German submarine which surfaced.
And because of it, the ship had to make a long detour.
Because as you well know, the-- the Germans, they dominated pretty much for a given period of time the Mediterranean seas with their submarines.

So that was pretty scary because at the time, the-- many of the refugees, they couldn't accept that idea that they-- they got out of the-- Germany, et cetera, alive or Poland or et cetera and to finish this way in the middle of the ocean, it was not quite acceptable.
And so there was discussions about it later on.
And the next scare that we had was there was a very rough sea, and the was really waving from side to side.
Long waves would come to the ship and sweep the ship.

And many people were out because in the-- I was one of those that I couldn't stand these-- these waves, you know, and I was relieving myself with others when the-- a wave took a hold of one-- a young child, a young refugee, and swept him across.
And I jumped and I grabbed a hold of this guy and I-- I-- I-- you might say I saved him from the possibility of going overboard.
And his father then came to me and wanted to kiss my feet.
It was so touching.
I didn't allow him that.
But there were some scary moments, but we ended up ultimately arriving well in Palestine, in Eretz Israel.

Well, a lot of-- of these children that left Portugal I believe was done through the influence-- other than the American Joint, the HIAS and the Jewish Community of Lisbon-- there was another group, the Youth Aliyah movement.
And this group was-- they-- I don't know if you can call it the brainchild is the right world of an American lady, a Jewish lady, by the name of Henrietta Szold, S-Z-O-L-D.
She was referred to as the mother of the Youth Aliyah movement.
And again, I was very privileged, very honorable, to meet with her personally at the Maon [INAUDIBLE] in Haifa.
Maon is spelled M-A-O-N, Maon [INAUDIBLE].
Because I was already over the age of the groups that they like to keep in the Youth Aliyah movement.
So instead of going with them to the camp, I was sent to Kibbutz Beit Alpha and I stayed there for a little while before I left.
Oh, absolutely.
I mean, they didn't-- they didn't come to mean and influence me personally.
I wanted to go, my brothers wanted to go.
My father and mother didn't want us to go at the time because, you know, we were a very close-knit family.
But I-- I said, no, I gotta go.
I have to go.
What influenced me even perhaps not less so was when I was taking Hebrew lessons at Mrs.
Torjman, ?] she had pictures at the willing wall of Jerusalem, of [INAUDIBLE] and many other pictures of Eretz Israel in her kitchen wall.
And I always wanted to go and see the-- the Wailing Wall.
But I was influenced-- when I saw that so many children were leaving for Eretz, I wanted to go to Eretz, too.
My brother and my cousin, Nissim.
He died in Israel, too.
